draft.introduction: Book 1 of Thomas à Kempis’s Imitation of Christ (c. 1420) is the most widely read devotional work in Christianity after the Bible. It counsels the interior life, humility, and detachment from worldly vanity in direct, unadorned prose.

translation.original_ref: Thomas à Kempis, Imitation of Christ
translation.text: He who follows me walks not in darkness, says the Lord. By these words of Christ we are admonished to imitate his life and manners, if we wish to be truly enlightened and to be delivered from all blindness of heart. Let therefore our chief endeavor be to meditate upon the life of Jesus Christ. What profit is it to discourse profoundly of the Trinity if you lack humility and are thereby displeasing to the Trinity?
translation.original_text: [Latin: Qui sequitur me non ambulat in tenebris, dicit Dominus]

translator_notes.note: The opening’s rebuke of speculative theology in favor of practical devotion reflects the Devotio Moderna movement’s emphasis on inner transformation over intellectual achievement. The question “What profit is it to discourse profoundly of the Trinity if you lack humility?” challenges the entire Scholastic enterprise from the perspective of lived holiness.
